mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M135i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M135i engine costs £3,800–£7,500 supply only depending on generation. F20 N55 engines start around £4,500, while F40 B48 units start around £3,800. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M135i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which generation you have. The F20/F21 N55 is a proven straight-six — reliable but with known weak points (HPFP, chargepipe, VANOS solenoids). The F40 B48 is more modern and generally more reliable, but the car itself is a very different proposition — front-/all-wheel-drive and four-cylinder.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M135i?

acceptedAnswer.text: F20/F21 N55: HPFP failure, chargepipe failure (plastic cracks), VANOS solenoid wear. F40 B48: less common issues, but cooling system problems and occasional timing chain vigilance are noted.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M135i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and which generation. Against a well-kept £18,000 F20, a £6,000 recon is 33% — worth it. Against a tired £8,000 F20 with other issues, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold. F40 values are higher — a £6,000 recon against a £30,000 car is 20% — definitely worth it.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M135i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. F20/F21 (2012-2019): N55B30A. F40 (2019-present): B48A20O1. The chassis code is the easiest tell: F20/F21 is the rear-drive hatch, F40 is the front-drive hatch.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the HPFP on the N55, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — HPFP replacement is a well-understood repair costing £600–£1,200. It's the N55's most common weak point. If you experience long cranking or hesitation, don't ignore it — but it's a straightforward fix, not an engine killer.

rows.eraNote: The original rear-wheel-drive M135i — a BMW purist's favourite. HPFP and chargepipe the weak points.
The front-/all-wheel-drive generation — a genuinely different car under the same badge. More reliable, less character.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B48 generation (F40), which has lower parts availability. Older F20 N55 engines typically run lower, with used examples starting around £2,500. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an M135i engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

problems.title: High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure — N55 (F20/F21)
problems.symptoms: Long cranking on startup, hesitation under acceleration, engine stalling, engine warning light, reduced power.
problems.recommendation: The N55's HPFP is its most notorious weak point. If you experience long cranking or hesitation, don't ignore it — a failing HPFP can lead to lean running and catalytic converter damage. The replacement is well-understood and well-documented. This applies exclusively to the F20/F21 M135i.

failureLink.label: Read the full HPFP failure guide

problems.title: Chargepipe Failure — N55 (F20/F21)
problems.symptoms: Loss of boost, whistling noise from the engine bay, engine warning light, reduced power.
problems.recommendation: The N55's plastic chargepipe is prone to cracking, especially on tuned cars. The solution is an aftermarket metal chargepipe — fit once and forget. This is exclusively an F20/F21 issue; the F40 B48 uses a revised design.

failureLink.label: Read the full chargepipe failure guide

problems.title: VANOS Solenoid Wear — N55 (F20/F21)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, reduced power at low RPM, hesitation, engine warning light with VANOS codes.
problems.recommendation: VANOS solenoid wear is common on higher-mileage N55 engines. The fix is relatively simple — cleaning or replacing the solenoids — but ignoring it can lead to timing issues. Regular oil changes with BMW LL-01 approved oil reduce the risk.

failureLink.label: Read the full VANOS failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This M135i? Check These First
buyIf: * F20/F21: Documented HPFP and chargepipe replacement history (aftermarket metal chargepipe preferred)
* F20/F21: Full service history with BMW LL-01 approved oil
* F40: Full service history and no evidence of cooling system issues
* Both: No cold-start rattle, misfires, or hesitation
avoidIf: * F20/F21: No proof of HPFP or chargepipe maintenance — these are whens, not ifs
* F20/F21: Poor service history — the N55 is unforgiving of neglect
* F40: Cooling system issues with no documented repair
* Both: Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M135i badge spans two completely different cars — the rear-wheel-drive, straight-six purist's F20 and the front-/all-wheel-drive, four-cylinder F40. The N55 is a legend with known weak points; the B48 is more reliable but less characterful. Buy the one that matches your priorities — but buy on documented history either way.

cta.label: → Compare M135i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW M135i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or hesitation (N55: HPFP warning; B48: timing chain vigilance)
* Service History — including which engine era (N55 for F20/F21, B48 for F40) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Chargepipe Check (F20/F21) — Has the plastic chargepipe been replaced with an aftermarket metal unit?
* HPFP Check (F20/F21) — Any history of long cranking, hesitation, or HPFP replacement?
* Smoke Check — White smoke can indicate turbo or cooling system issues
* VANOS Operation (F20/F21) — Any rough idle or hesitation at low RPM?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved HPFP, chargepipe, VANOS, or cooling system issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
